agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of the Shipment aggregate from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental domain model change affecting the core bounded context of shipment fulfillment, with downstream impact on 2M existing records and external partner API contracts.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods being transported from origin to one or more delivery destinations. The aggregate root that owns Recipients."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"A party receiving part or all of a Shipment. Identified by name, address, phone number, and now delivery preferences. Transitions from a value object embedded in Shipment to a first-class entity within the Shipment aggregate."},{"term":"Delivery Preferences","definition":"Recipient-specific instructions governing how and when delivery should occur (e.g., time windows, signature requirements, safe-place authorization, contactless delivery)."},{"term":"Primary Recipient","definition":"The designated main recipient on a multi-recipient shipment, used for backward compatibility and as the default contact for operational decisions."},{"term":"Shipment Split","definition":"The potential operational concept where a single logical shipment with multiple recipients results in multiple physical delivery stops or sub-shipments."},{"term":"Partner API Contract","definition":"The external-facing data format currently exposing a single recipient per shipment, consumed by external partner systems."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have at least one Recipient at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A shipment without a recipient has no delivery destination and is physically undeliverable. This is a data integrity constraint — the system is corrupt if violated.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to create a Shipment with zero Recipients, or remove the last Recipient from a Shipment, must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Each Recipient within a Shipment must have a valid name, address, and phone number.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"These are the minimum fields required for a courier to attempt delivery. Without them, the physical delivery process cannot proceed.","testable_assertion":"Persisting a Recipient with a null or empty name, address, or phone number must be rejected by the domain model."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Each Recipient within a Shipment may have independent delivery preferences.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Delivery preferences are a business policy feature. A VP could decide to enforce uniform preferences per shipment.","testable_assertion":"Two Recipients on the same Shipment can have different delivery preference values and both are honored independently."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Existing single-recipient shipments must remain valid and queryable after the model migration without data loss or behavioral change.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Corrupting or losing 2M production records violates data integrity. Existing shipments must continue to function identically.","testable_assertion":"After migration, every pre-existing shipment returns exactly the same recipient data (name, address, phone) as before migration."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"External partner APIs must continue to receive shipment data in a format they can consume without breaking their integrations.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a contractual/partnership obligation, not a physical constraint. Partners can be migrated on a timeline, but the obligation is real.","testable_assertion":"Partner API responses for single-recipient shipments must be byte-compatible with the pre-change format. Multi-recipient shipments must have a defined serialization strategy agreed with partners."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A Shipment must designate exactly one Primary Recipient when multiple Recipients exist.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Operational decisions (who to call when delivery fails, who signs for the whole shipment, return-to-sender contact) require a single point of contact. Without this, the courier is blocked.","testable_assertion":"A Shipment with N>1 Recipients must have exactly one flagged as primary. Attempting to save without a primary, or with two primaries, must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Each Recipient on a Shipment must have a unique address. Duplicate addresses within the same Shipment are rejected.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a data quality policy to prevent accidental duplicate entries. A business decision could relax this for legitimate use cases like sending to the same address with different contact persons.","testable_assertion":"Adding two Recipients with identical addresses to the same Shipment must produce a validation error."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"The creator provides two or more Recipients with valid name, address, phone, and delivery preferences","then":"The Shipment is persisted with all Recipients, the first (or explicitly designated) Recipient is marked Primary, and each Recipient's delivery preferences are stored independently."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An existing single-recipient Shipment from before migration","when":"The Shipment is loaded from the database","then":"It is represented as a Shipment with exactly one Recipient who is also the Primary Recipient, with delivery preferences defaulted. No data loss occurs."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment exists","when":"An external partner API requests this Shipment's data and the partner has NOT been migrated to the new format","then":"The API returns only the Primary Recipient in the legacy single-recipient format, preserving backward compatibility."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A Shipment has three Recipients","when":"A user attempts to remove all three Recipients","then":"The system rejects the operation, keeping at least the last Recipient intact, and returns a validation error."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Shipment has two Recipients","when":"The Primary Recipient is removed","then":"The system either rejects the removal until a new Primary is designated, or automatically promotes the remaining Recipient to Primary."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment is in transit","when":"Delivery fails for one Recipient but succeeds for another","then":"The Shipment tracks per-Recipient delivery status independently. The overall Shipment status reflects a partial delivery state."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"The data migration runs against 2M existing shipments","when":"Migration completes","then":"All 2M shipments have exactly one Recipient marked as Primary, with delivery preferences set to system defaults. Zero records are lost or corrupted. Migration is reversible."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment has 50 Recipients — what is the upper bound?","expected_behavior":"The system must enforce a maximum recipient count per shipment (to be defined by product). Without a cap, operational complexity and UI/UX degrade. Recommend a sensible default (e.g., 10) that can be configured."},{"scenario":"Two Recipients on the same Shipment have the same phone number but different addresses","expected_behavior":"This is allowed. Phone number uniqueness is not required within a Shipment — the same person may receive deliveries at multiple locations."},{"scenario":"A partner API webhook fires for a multi-recipient Shipment before the partner has been migrated","expected_behavior":"Webhook payload must use the legacy format with the Primary Recipient only. Event schema versioning must be in place."},{"scenario":"Recipient is added to a Shipment that is already in 'Delivered' status","expected_behavior":"Reject the operation. Adding recipients to a completed shipment violates the shipment lifecycle. Recipients can only be modified while shipment is in a pre-terminal state."},{"scenario":"Data migration fails midway through the 2M records","expected_behavior":"Migration must be idempotent and resumable. Partially migrated state must not break reads. A rollback path must exist."},{"scenario":"A Shipment is created with one Recipient — is Primary explicitly required?","expected_behavior":"When there is exactly one Recipient, that Recipient is implicitly the Primary. The system should auto-assign Primary status without requiring the caller to specify it."},{"scenario":"Courier arrives and two Recipients at different addresses both expect the same physical package","expected_behavior":"The domain must clarify whether multi-recipient means multiple delivery stops for the SAME goods (e.g., legal copies) or splitting goods across recipients. This distinction affects routing, cost, and SLA."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Migration and API versioning have direct engineering cost. Multi-recipient shipments may enable new revenue streams (group orders, corporate multi-drop). Incorrect migration risks data loss affecting billing, insurance claims, and compliance — potentially millions in liability.","operational":"Courier routing becomes more complex with multiple stops per shipment. Dispatch algorithms, SLA calculations, and delivery tracking all need to accommodate per-recipient status. Support teams need new tooling to view and manage multi-recipient shipments. Migration of 2M records requires a carefully planned rollout with monitoring.","user":"Senders gain flexibility for multi-drop shipments. Recipients get personalized delivery preferences. However, UX complexity increases — the shipment creation flow, tracking pages, and notification systems all must handle N recipients gracefully. Poor UX could reduce adoption.","risk":"HIGH — This is a structural change to the core aggregate. Risks include: (1) data migration corruption of 2M records, (2) breaking external partner integrations, (3) inconsistent partial-delivery states creating support burden, (4) performance degradation on queries that assumed 1:1 relationship. Each risk requires explicit mitigation.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"External Partners","impact":"Their integrations break if API contract changes without versioning and migration support. Must be communicated early with a deprecation timeline."},{"group":"Couriers / Drivers","impact":"Multi-stop shipments change routing, workload estimation, and delivery confirmation flows. Driver apps must support per-recipient status updates."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Must handle new failure modes (partial delivery, wrong recipient marked primary, preference conflicts). Need updated tooling and training."},{"group":"Senders (Shippers)","impact":"Gain multi-recipient capability but face more complex shipment creation. API clients and UI both need updates."},{"group":"Recipients","impact":"Gain personalized delivery preferences. May experience confusion if tracking shows partial delivery of a shared shipment."},{"group":"Data / Analytics Team","impact":"Reporting and dashboards built on 1:1 assumption break. Metrics like 'deliveries per shipment' change meaning."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Pricing model may need revision — is a multi-recipient shipment one charge or N charges? Cost allocation per recipient needs definition."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The product team's requirement is clear directionally (1:N recipients with preferences), but critical business semantics are undefined. The spec does not clarify whether multi-recipient means multiple delivery stops for the same goods, splitting goods, or something else. Delivery preferences are mentioned but not enumerated. The partner API migration strategy is unspecified. Pricing implications are unaddressed. These gaps prevent high confidence in a complete domain model.","high_confidence_areas":["The 1:N structural change from embedded value object to entity collection is well-understood","Backward compatibility for 2M existing records is a hard requirement","External partner API backward compatibility is essential","At-least-one-recipient invariant is clear"],"low_confidence_areas":["What 'delivery preferences' specifically includes — time windows? signature? contactless? safe place?","Whether multi-recipient means multi-drop (same goods, multiple stops) or goods-splitting (different items to different people)","Pricing model for multi-recipient shipments — per shipment or per recipient","Upper bound on recipients per shipment","Partner API versioning strategy and deprecation timeline","Whether partial delivery is a valid terminal state or requires resolution"],"assumptions":["Multi-recipient means multiple delivery stops for the same logical shipment, not splitting physical goods","A Primary Recipient concept is needed for backward compatibility and operational decisions","Delivery preferences are per-recipient, not per-shipment","The migration will be online (no downtime) given production scale"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The term 'multi-recipient shipment' is ambiguous. It could mean: (A) the same physical package is delivered to multiple addresses in sequence (e.g., legal documents requiring multiple signatories), (B) a shipment's contents are split across recipients (each gets different items), or (C) a single delivery address with multiple named recipients (e.g., office building). Each interpretation has radically different implications for routing, pricing, tracking, and the physical delivery process.","affected_stakeholders":["Couriers","Senders","Recipients","Finance","Dispatch/Routing"],  "options":["A: Multi-drop — same goods visit N addresses in sequence","B: Goods-split — shipment contents divided among N recipients at different addresses","C: Co-located — multiple named contacts at same destination","D: Hybrid — support multiple modes with a shipment-level type indicator"],"recommendation":"Clarify with product team before implementation. Recommend option D (hybrid with type indicator) for maximum flexibility, but the domain model, routing, and pricing differ significantly per mode. Cannot define correct acceptance criteria without this answer."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Delivery preferences' is referenced but never enumerated. The set of valid preferences determines the Recipient entity schema, validation rules, and operational impact. Without knowing if preferences include time windows, signature requirements, safe-place authorization, contactless delivery, or other options, the domain model cannot be fully specified.","affected_stakeholders":["Recipients","Couriers","Product Team","Engineering"],"options":["A: Define a minimal initial set (time window, signature required, safe place) and extend later","B: Make preferences a flexible key-value store","C: Wait for full enumeration from product"],"recommendation":"Option A — define a concrete minimal set to unblock implementation while keeping the schema extensible. But product must confirm the initial set."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Pricing model for multi-recipient shipments is unspecified. A shipment with 3 recipients at 3 addresses has fundamentally different cost structure than a single-recipient shipment. Is it billed as one shipment, as N deliveries, or with a base + per-stop surcharge?","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Senders","Partners"],"options":["A: Single shipment price regardless of recipient count","B: Base price + per-additional-recipient surcharge","C: Priced as N independent deliveries bundled under one tracking number"],"recommendation":"This must be decided by Finance/Product before the feature ships. It affects the Shipment aggregate (must it carry pricing per recipient?) and all downstream billing systems."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
